input.prompt:
I just upgraded my .NET MAUI app from .NET 9 to .NET 10. On iOS, my content
is now going behind the status bar and the Dynamic Island notch area. In .NET 9
I was using `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` and it worked fine. What's the correct
way to handle this in .NET 10? Show me the XAML.

way to handle this in .NET 10? Show me the XAML.

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET MAUI migration issue from .NET 9 to .NET 10 regarding safe area handling on iOS. This is a known breaking change in .NET 10 MAUI.

In .NET 10, the `ios:Page.UseSafeArea` attached property was removed/deprecated. The new approach is to use `Page.IgnoreSafeArea` or padding-based approaches.

Actually, let me think more carefully. In .NET MAUI for .NET 9, `ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True"` was the platform-specific way to handle safe areas. 

In .NET 10, Microsoft changed the safe area behavior significantly—safe area insets are now applied by default, and the API shifted to use the `IgnoreSafeArea` property instead, essentially flipping the default behavior. The migration path typically involves either using padding-based approaches or the new safe area APIs that replaced the old platform-specific property. I'm uncertain about the exact .NET 10 MAUI API changes, but from what I understand, safe area handling shifted to a cross-platform model where `UseSafeArea` was replaced—pages now handle safe area automatically by default, though I'm not entirely confident in the specific new property names or whether content going behind the status bar requires a different configuration approach. might need to ensure your page layout uses the correct container. Wait - the user mentioned content is going behind the status bar after upgrading, so the old `UseSafeArea="True"` isn't working anymore. This suggests either the property was removed or renamed in .NET 10 MAUI. I'm thinking the framework shifted from a platform-specific approach to a cross-platform property, likely something like `IgnoreSafeArea` that works across all platforms instead of the iOS-specific namespace syntax. Let me provide the most practical solution based on how the API probably evolved. I'm realizing I need to step back and think about this more carefully. The `IgnoreSafeArea` property is actually a layout-level setting in MAUI, not a page-level one, so that's not the right direction either. For .NET 10, the most practical solution is probably to handle safe areas through layout padding or by checking if there's a newer cross-platform property that replaced the iOS-specific `UseSafeArea` approach. MAUI handling safe areas on iOS when `UseSafeArea` no longer works:

The recommended approach in .NET 10 would be to handle it via the page's padding. You can set the page padding to account for safe area insets programmatically, or use a handler/behavior.

In .NET 10, the `ios:Page.UseSafeArea` platform-specific property was removed, and safe area insets are now applied to pages by default instead. If content is still overlapping the status bar, it's likely a layout issue or the user needs to explicitly set padding on their content.

Setting up the page with padding... In .NET 10 MAUI, safe area handling became the default behavior, so the old `UseSafeArea` property is likely no longer needed or has been replaced with a different approach. If content is appearing behind the status bar after upgrading, it could be due to custom handlers, a preview bug, or needing to remove the deprecated property entirely and let the framework handle it automatically. I'm realizing I should clarify what I actually know versus what I'm inferring. The core change in .NET MAUI is that the iOS-specific `UseSafeArea` property was replaced with a cross-platform `IgnoreSafeArea` property on the Page itself, where `False` (the default) respects the safe area and `True` ignores it. So the migration path is straightforward: remove the iOS namespace declaration and use the new property instead.

But since the user says this isn't working, I'm wondering if the issue is that simply removing the old property doesn't automatically apply the safe area padding they expect, or if there's a different mechanism in .NET 10 for handling safe area insets—maybe through a `SafeAreaView` component or manual padding adjustments based on platform-specific values. I'm realizing I need to be more direct here—I should look up the actual .NET 10 documentation to give an accurate answer rather than guessing at the API.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Contents of https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/maui/ios/platform-specifics/page-safe-area-layout?view=net-maui-10.0:
By default, .NET Multi-platform App UI (.NET MAUI) apps automatically position page content on an area of the screen that is safe for all devices. This is known as the safe area layout guide, and ensures that content isn't clipped by rounded device corners, the home indicator, or the sensor housing on some iPhone models.

Important

In .NET 10, use the [SafeAreaEdges](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentpage.safeareaedges#microsoft-maui-controls-contentpage-safeareaedges) property (also available on [Layout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.layout), [ScrollView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.scrollview), [ContentView](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.contentview), and [Border](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.border)) to precisely control safe area behavior. The iOS-specific `Page.UseSafeArea` remains for existing apps, but new code should prefer `SafeAreaEdges`.

## Modern safe area guidance (.NET 10+)

### Choose the safe area behavior with SafeAreaEdges

`SafeAreaEdges` accepts the following values from [SafeAreaEdges](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.safeareaedges): `Default`, `None`, `Container`, `SoftInput`, and `All`.

*   Keep content within all safe areas (avoid bars, notch, and keyboard):
    
    ```
    <ContentPage SafeAreaEdges="All">
            <!-- content stays within safe area -->
    </ContentPage>
    ```
    
*   Draw edge-to-edge (no safe area padding):
    
    ```
    <ContentPage SafeAreaEdges="None">
            <!-- content can extend behind bars and notch -->
    </ContentPage>
    ```
    
*   Keep out of the keyboard (soft input), but allow under top/bottom bars/notch:
    
    ```
    <ScrollView SafeAreaEdges="SoftInput">
            <!-- content can go under bars/notch but avoids keyboard overlap -->
    </ScrollView>
    ```
    
*   Keep out of top/bottom bars and notch, but allow under the keyboard:
    
    ```
    <Grid SafeAreaEdges="Container">
            <!-- content avoids bars/notch; may extend under keyboard -->
    </Grid>
    ```
    

The default value is `Default`, which maps to edge-to-edge behavior (equivalent to `None`) unless overridden by platform conventions.

You can set `SafeAreaEdges` in C# as well:

```
using Microsoft.Maui;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls;

// On a page
this.SafeAreaEdges = SafeAreaEdges.All;

// On a layout
myGrid.SafeAreaEdges = SafeAreaEdges.Container;

// Keyboard-aware
myScrollView.SafeAreaEdges = SafeAreaEdges.SoftInput;
```

### Reading the current safe area insets

To read the current safe area insets on a page at runtime, use the iOS-specific configuration API:

```
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;

var insets = On<iOS>().SafeAreaInsets(); // Microsoft.Maui.Thickness
```

Tip

Safe area insets can change at runtime (for example, rotation or status bar changes). Update layout when insets change.

For more information, see:

*   [Safe area layout](../../user-interface/safe-area?view=net-maui-10.0)
*   [Safe area enhancements in .NET 10](../../whats-new/dotnet-10?view=net-maui-10.0#safearea-enhancements)

This iOS platform-specific enables the safe area layout guide, if it's previously been disabled, and is consumed in XAML by setting the `Page.UseSafeArea` attached property to `true`:

```
<ContentPage ...
             xmlns:ios="clr-namespace:Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;assembly=Microsoft.Maui.Controls"
             ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True">
    <StackLayout>
        ...
    </StackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

Alternatively, it can be consumed from C# using the fluent API:

```
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;

On<iOS>().SetUseSafeArea(true);
```

The `Page.On<iOS>` method specifies that this platform-specific will only run on iOS. The `Page.SetUseSafeArea` method, in the `Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific` namespace, controls whether the safe area layout guide is disabled.

Note

The [Layout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.layout) class defines a [IgnoreSafeArea](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.layout.ignoresafearea#microsoft-maui-controls-layout-ignoresafearea) property that ensures that content is positioned on an area of the screen that is safe for all iOS devices. This property can be set to `true` on any layout class, such as a [Grid](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.grid) or [StackLayout](/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.maui.controls.stacklayout), to perform the equivalent of this platform-specific.

data.content:
In .NET 10, `ios:Page.UseSafeArea` is replaced by the new **cross-platform** `SafeAreaEdges` property. The default is now edge-to-edge (`None`), which is why your content goes behind the notch.

**Before (.NET 9):**
```xml
<ContentPage xmlns:ios="clr-namespace:Microsoft.Maui.Controls.PlatformConfiguration.iOSSpecific;assembly=Microsoft.Maui.Controls"
             ios:Page.UseSafeArea="True">
```

**After (.NET 10):**
```xml
<ContentPage SafeAreaEdges="All">
    <!-- content stays within safe area on all platforms -->
</ContentPage>
```

Key values for `SafeAreaEdges`:

| Value | Behavior |
|---|---|
| `All` | Avoids bars, notch, **and** keyboard |
| `Container` | Avoids bars/notch, but allows under keyboard |
| `SoftInput` | Allows under bars/notch, avoids keyboard |
| `None` | Full edge-to-edge (the new default) |

It also works on `Grid`, `ScrollView`, `ContentView`, and `Border` — so you can apply it granularly to individual layouts instead of the whole page.
data.reasoningText:
Now I have the accurate information. Let me summarize the answer for the user.

In .NET 10, the new cross-platform `SafeAreaEdges` property replaces the iOS-specific `ios:Page.UseSafeArea`. The default is `Default` which maps to edge-to-edge (equivalent to `None`), which explains why the user's content goes behind the status bar/notch.

The fix is to use `SafeAreaEdges="All"` on the ContentPage.
